data.title: NIO: Don't Sell The Rally Yet
data.description:
NIO Inc. (NYSE:NIO) submitted a mixed earnings sheet on Tuesday that showed an EPS beat but a revenue miss. NIO generated 9% year-over-year top-line growth and benefited from a continual vehicle margin increase: in the second quarter, the electric vehicle company achieved a vehicle margin of 10.3% (+0.1 PP Q/Q) but continued to suffer from high operating losses in its core business. I continue to see potential for NIO to expand its valuation if the start-up succeeds in narrowing its operating losses while slowly building up its vehicle margins. Since shares of NIO are still very attractively valued, I also see a risk profile that is skewed to the upside.

Previous Rating

I rated shares of NIO a strong buy in my last work on the EV company—Don't Give Up Just Yet—mainly because I saw strong momentum for the firm's low-cost EV brand, Onvo. Since then, shares of the EV start-up have surged ~80%, but I see more upside left if NIO manages to lower its operating losses while maintaining the current rate of delivery growth. Further, NIO submitted a strong delivery outlook for Q3'25, and shares are underpriced relative to other EV start-ups in the industry group.

NIO Reported Mixed Q2 Results

NIO submitted an earnings scorecard for the second quarter on Tuesday that beat on EPS but missed on the top line: NIO reported adjusted earnings of $(0.26) per share, which surpassed the firm's consensus estimate by $0.05 per share. The top line reached $2.66B and fell short of the average prediction by $102.9M.

Despite the revenue estimate miss, NIO delivered strong results in terms of its top line and its vehicle margins in the second quarter. NIO generated a vehicle margin of 10.3% in Q2'25, which reflects a 0.1% Q/Q margin increase. For comparison, XPeng (XPEV), which has overtaken its electric vehicle rivals in terms of delivery growth in recent months, reported a vehicle margin of 14.3% (+3.8 PP Q/Q) in Q2'25, while Li Auto (LI) is still leading the industry group with a vehicle margin of 19.4% (-0.4 PP Q/Q).

Because of higher margins (on both a vehicle and gross profit level) and a higher delivery volume compared to Q2'24, NIO generated 19.0B Chinese Yuan ($2.7B), showing 9% year-over-year growth. NIO did make considerable progress in the second quarter in ramping up its deliveries, mainly due to the company's new brand launches, Onvo and Firefly, which is where the EV company expects its momentum to continue.

In Q2'25, NIO delivered 72,056 electric vehicles, showing 25.6% year-over-year growth. In August, NIO delivered 31,305 EVs, a record monthly delivery volume for the EV start-up, showing 55.2% year-over-year growth. This growth was chiefly driven by Onvo, the firm's low-cost EV brand, which the company launched last year in order to woe more price-conscious younger buyers of EVs. In August, Onvo deliveries totaled 16,434 EVs (+175% month-over-month), representing about 53% of all deliveries during the month. Firefly, which is another low-cost compact EV brand for urban buyers that NIO kicked off in April 2025, is also seeing strong growth: Firefly deliveries totaled 4,346 EVs in August, up 83.7% month-over-month.

Onvo-driven delivery growth is the still the number one reason for me why I continue to recommend shares of NIO—despite a nearly 80% price gain since my last recommendation.

Unfortunately, NIO has one major weakness: the company is losing a ton of money on its EV operations. In the second quarter, NIO generated an operating loss of 4.9B Chinese Yuan ($685.2M), which showed a decline rate of 9% year-over-year, but NIO's losses are still material, especially in comparison to other EV companies. Li Auto, as an example, generated operating income of 827M Chinese Yuan ($115.4M), while XPeng's financial statements showed a loss of 934.6M Chinese Yuan ($130.5M). From a profitability perspective, NIO has the worst setup and needs to urgently improve its margins in order to sustain recent valuation gains.

NIO's Outlook for the Third Quarter

Despite high operating losses, the good news for the EV start-up is that NIO expects strong delivery momentum for Onvo- and Firefly-branded electric vehicles to last into the second half of the year. According to NIO's earnings scorecard, the company expects to see a total delivery volume of between 87,000 and 91,000 vehicles in Q3'25, led by Onvo, which would show an increase of up to 47.1% compared to the year-earlier period.

NIO's Valuation and Catalysts for an Upside Revaluation

One of the single most attractive features of an investment in NIO is that the company is trading at a price-to-revenue ratio, based off of FY 2026 estimates, of less than 1.0X—which makes it an absolute bargain in the EV industry group, in my opinion.

NIO is currently valued at a price-to-revenue ratio of 0.91X, which establishes the EV start-up as the cheapest EV play in the electric vehicle industry group. XPeng, which currently has the strongest momentum in terms of deliveries, is trading at a forward P/S ratio of 1.23X, while Li Auto is still the best deal, in my opinion, given its considerable margin advantage and low P/S ratio of 0.92X.

In my opinion, NIO still has upside revaluation potential as it executes on its Onvo-driven growth strategy. If NIO can sustain its growth momentum in its low-cost EV segment, I believe the electric vehicle start-up could revalue to its historical P/S ratio of 1.31X—which implies a fair value in the neighborhood of $9.42/share.

NIO has upside revaluation potential if a number of conditions are met:

  • The EV start-up sustains its Onvo- and Firefly-related delivery momentum as consumers are shifting over to lower-cost EV brands
  • NIO continues to make vehicle margin gains and catches up to its EV competition, especially Li Auto
  • NIO lowers its operating losses and gradually transitions to an improving profitability profile
  • New EV product launches could help the company accelerate its delivery momentum: Firefly deliveries, for example, started in Europe last month and could be rapidly scaled given the market's taste for lower-priced EVs. With Firefly already having significant momentum, international expansion could be an accelerant for NIO's delivery growth.

NIO's Risks

NIO has material risks regarding its profitability trajectory, as the company has by far the highest operating losses in the industry group. The EV firm could therefore be forced to issue more stock and dilute shareholders as a result. I would be willing to adjust my opinion and my stock rating for the Chinese electric vehicle company if NIO were to see a contraction in its vehicle margins or failed to reduce its operating losses in the quarters ahead.

Closing Thoughts

NIO is seeing strong Onvo-driven electric vehicle delivery growth, and Firefly is off to a good start since it started to deliver low-cost compact EVs in April of 2025. NIO also submitted a strong outlook for deliveries in the third quarter, which implies that the EV start-up expects its current Onvo and Firefly momentum to continue, especially as European expansion picks up steam. While NIO missed revenue estimates on Tuesday, the EV firm had a decent quarter overall, defined by strong delivery growth and a small sequential improvement in vehicle margins. On the negative side, NIO still has to deal with high operating losses. I believe shares are still very attractively priced given the delivery backdrop, trading for less than 1.0X forward revenue, and I see a favorable risk profile for investors.

data.title: American Century One Choice Target Date Portfolios Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
All 10 One Choice Portfolios posted positive returns in the second quarter.

Market Environment

Large caps ended the quarter at an all-time high. Major market stocks initially fell sharply after the Liberation Day tariff announcement, only to rebound after the tariffs were postponed. U.S. large-cap stocks rallied on the strength of a resumption of the artificial intelligence stock trade. During the quarter, growth stocks outperformed value, and non-U.S. developed and emerging markets stocks outperformed U.S. stocks.

The economic environment was mostly positive. Inflation moderated, and as of July 1, the Atlanta Federal Reserve’s (Fed’s) GDPNow projected annualized real gross domestic product (GDP) growth of 2.5% for the second quarter. Consumer confidence rebounded in June, posting a gain for the first time in six months.

Bond prices rose, producing solid total returns for the quarter. Slowing inflation, modest economic growth and investors looking ahead to the likelihood of Fed rate cuts later in the year led to gains. Solid corporate profit growth helped support demand for both high-yield and investment-grade corporate bonds amid moderating economic growth and inflation.

Key Contributors

Growth-oriented holdings led the way. Equity holdings such as Focused Dynamic Growth, International Small-Mid Cap, Heritage, Growth and Select posted robust gains over the quarter, highlighting the strong performance of growth stocks.

All bond positions advanced. Across fixed-income holdings, Emerging Markets Debt and High Income were top performers.

Key Detractors

Only two absolute detractors for the quarter. In a strong environment for large- and mid-cap growth stocks, Small Cap Dividend (in shorter-dated portfolios) and Small Cap Value (in longer-dated ones) were the only funds with negative returns.

Dynamic Risk Management Process

Tariff threats de-escalate, but uncertainty remains high. The second quarter finished with equity markets reaching all-time highs, after rebounding strongly on optimism for trade deals. However, businesses and consumers know that, unless something changes, tariffs that were paused in May could return. Therefore, uncertainty remains high, and companies are reluctant to invest and hire new workers. In May, we learned that first-quarter GDP declined slightly due to a huge surge in imports, as businesses sought to front-load purchases in anticipation of tariffs.

Similarly, the outlook for inflation and the economy is unclear, depending on the timing and level of any new tariffs. If consumers fear higher inflation, they are likely to adjust their buying behaviors and demand higher wages, which could push inflation higher. As a result, the Fed continues to wait for clearer signals about the impact of tariffs before cutting interest rates.

Dynamic risk management remains at a conservative positioning. At the end of June, elevated stock valuations and continuing economic and policy uncertainty have kept our outlook for stocks muted. As a result, we are modestly overweight bonds relative to the benchmark and underweight equities, most notably in the shorter-dated One Choice Portfolios.

References to specific securities are for illustrative purposes only and are not intended as recommendations to purchase or sell securities. Opinions and estimates offered constitute our judgment and, along with other portfolio data, are subject to change without notice.

Adjustable-Rate Mortgages (ARMs): Mortgages in which the interest rate is adjusted periodically according to a specific index.

Agency Securities (Agencies): Debt securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Agency MBS: Mortgage-backed securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Alternative Minimum Tax (AMT): A parallel tax system that was created to keep high income individuals from avoiding taxes through various deductions and exemptions.

Asset-Backed Securities (ABS): A form of securitized debt backed by loan assets such as auto loans, student loans, and credit card debt.

Basis Points (BPS): Used to express percentage values that are carried out to two decimal places. One basis point equals 0.01%.

Breakeven Rate: Yield difference between nominal Treasury notes and TIPS; indicates the market’s expectations for inflation.

Carry-Oriented Currencies: Higher-yielding currencies of countries where interest rates are generally higher than those of countries with lower-yielding currencies. These higher-yielding currencies are targeted for “carry trades,” where investors borrow money in a low-interest rate currency and invest in a higher yielding currency, potentially profiting from the difference in interest rates.

Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs): A form of securitized debt, typically backed by pools of corporate loans and their payments.

Collateralized Mortgage Obligations (CMOs): A form of structured, securitized debt derived from mortgage-backed securities, typically backed by pools of residential mortgages and their payments.

Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities (CMBS): Securities representing ownership in pools of commercial real estate loans used to finance the construction and improvement of income-producing properties such as hotels, shopping centers, and office buildings.

Commercial Paper: Short-term debt issued by corporations to raise cash and to cover current expenses in anticipation of future revenues.

Commodity: Basic raw materials such as precious metals and natural resources.

Consumer Price Index (CPI): Published by the U.S. government, CPI is the most commonly used statistic to measure inflation in the U.S. economy. Core CPI excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile.

Contribution to Duration (CTD): A measure of bond portfolio risk that reflects a bond sector’s contribution to the overall duration of the portfolio.

Corporate Debt: Debt instruments issued by private corporations.

Covered Bonds: Debt securities backed by cash flows from pools of mortgages or public sector loans. The asset pools help secure or “cover” these bonds if the originating financial institution becomes insolvent.

Coupon Interest Rate: The interest rate that is assigned to an interest-paying fixed income security when it is issued.

Credit Analysis: An analysis of the financial strength of the issuer of a security, and the ability of that issuer to provide timely payment of interest and principal.

Credit Default Swap: Credit derivative contracts between two counterparties that can be used to hedge credit risk or speculate on the changes in the credit quality of a corporation or government entity.

Credit Quality: Measures (usually in terms of high or low) the ability of issuers of debt securities to make timely interest and principal payments.

Credit Risk: The risk that the inability of the issuers of debt securities to make payments will cause these securities to decline in value.

Currency Overlay: A financial trading strategy used to separate the management of currency risk from other portfolio strategies. A currency

overlay manager can seek to hedge the risk from adverse movements in exchange rates, and/or attempt to profit from tactical currency views.

Debt Service: The amount of money required within a given period to keep current on required/scheduled repayments of outstanding debt, including interest and principal.

Deflation: A decline in prices for goods, assets and services.

Derivatives: Securities whose performance and/or structure is derived from the performance and/or structure of other assets, interest rates or indexes.

Duration: A measure of the price sensitivity of a fixed income investment to changes in interest rates. The longer the duration, expressed in years, the more a fixed income investment’s price will change when interest rates change.

Emerging Markets (EM) Debt: Debt issued by countries whose economies are considered to be developing or emerging from underdevelopment.

Excess Return: The return of an investment portfolio minus the return of what is considered to be a relatively risk-free asset, such as a U.S. Treasury bill.

Exchange-Traded Fund (ETF): Represents a group of securities but is traded on an exchange like an individual stock.

Federal Funds Rate: An overnight interest rate banks charge each other for loans.

Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC): The committee that sets interest rate and credit policies for the Federal Reserve System, the U.S. central bank.

Federal Reserve (Fed): The U.S. central bank, responsible for monetary policies affecting the U.S. financial system and the economy.

Fundamental Factors: Economic or financial data used in determining asset value.

Futures Contracts (Futures): Agreements to buy or sell a specific amount of a commodity or financial instrument at a particular price on a stipulated future date.

General Obligation (GO) Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the full faith and credit of the issuer, including its taxing power.

Gross Domestic Product (GDP): A measure of the total economic output in goods and services for an economy.

Government-Sponsored Enterprises (GSEs): Privately owned corporations created by Congress to provide funding and help to reduce the cost of capital for certain borrowing sectors of the economy such as homeowners, students and farmers.

Headline Risk: Refers to the risk that a negative news media headline about one security issuer, incident or sector could affect the demand for and pricing of a much wider swath of securities.

Hedge: An investment designed to reduce the risk of an adverse price move in another investment. Often a hedge consists of taking an offsetting position in a related investment, such as a futures contract.

High-Yield (HY) Debt: Fixed income securities with lower credit quality and lower credit ratings. High-yield securities are rated below BBB-.

Hybrid Adjustable-Rate Mortgages (ARMs): Combine the characteristics of fixed-rate and adjustable-rate mortgages, with rates remaining fixed for a set period of time and then adjusting periodically according to a specific index.

Inflation: An economic condition of rising prices for goods, services and assets, or equivalently, a declining value of money. Core inflation excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile. It is the opposite of Deflation.

Inflation-Indexed Securities: Debt securities that offer returns adjusted for inflation. Typically, the principal of inflation-indexed securities is indexed to a widely used inflation measure and adjusted accordingly. Interest payments can be adjusted as well.

Interest Rate Risk: The risk that a fixed income investment’s value will change due to changes in interest rates.

Investment-Grade (IG) Debt: Fixed income securities with relatively high credit quality and credit ratings in the upper ranges of those provided by credit rating services. Using Standard & Poor’s ratings as the benchmark, investment-grade securities are those rated BBB- or higher.

Leverage: The use of financial instruments and/or borrowed capital to increase potential returns or to increase purchasing power.

Long Position: Typical ownership of an asset or investment that gives the owner the right to transfer ownership, the right to any income generated by the asset, and the right to any profits or losses due to value changes.

Long/Short Position: A “market neutral” investing strategy that involves taking long positions in assets that are expected to increase in value and short positions in assets that are expected to decrease in value.

Master Limited Partnerships (MLPs): Publicly traded, generally higher-yielding securities of enterprises that engage in certain businesses, usually pertaining to the use of natural resources.

Mortgage-Backed Securities (MBS): A form of securitized debt that represents ownership in pools of mortgage loans and their payments.

MSCI ACWI (All Country World Index): A free float-adjusted market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of developed and emerging markets.

Municipal Securities: Debt securities typically issued by or on behalf of U.S. state and local governments, their agencies or authorities to raise money for a variety of public purposes, including financing for state and local governments as well as financing for specific projects and public facilities.

Nominal Yield: The interest rate to par value that the bond issuer promises to pay bond purchasers.

Non-Agency Mortgage-Backed Securities: MBS are groups of home mortgages that are sold by the issuing banks and then packaged together into "pools" and sold as a single security. Non-agency MBS are issued by private entities, such as financial institutions.

Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE): This price deflator which comes from the Bureau of Economic Analysis' quarterly report on U.S. gross domestic product is based on a survey of businesses and is intended to capture the price changes in all final goods, no matter the purchaser. Because of its broader scope and certain differences in the methodology used to calculate the PCE price index, the Federal Reserve holds the PCE deflator as its preferred, consistent measure of inflation over time.

Quantitative Easing (QE): A form of monetary policy used by central banks to stimulate economic growth by purchasing domestic government securities to increase the domestic money supply, lower interest rates and encourage investors to make investments in riskier assets such as stocks and high-yield securities.

Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs): Securities that trade like stocks and invest in real estate through properties or mortgages.

Real Yield: A yield that has been adjusted to remove the effects of inflation.

Revenue Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the net revenues from the project or facility being financed.

S&P 500 Index: The S&P 500® Index is composed of 500 selected common stocks most of which are listed on the New York Stock Exchange. It is not an investment product available for purchase.

Secondary Market: A market where investors make purchases from other investors, rather than from the initial issuers.

Securitized Debt: Debt resulting from aggregating debt instruments into a pool of similar debts, then issuing new securities backed by the pool.

ABS, MBS, and CMOs are common forms of securitized debt.

Short Position: Refers to the sale of an asset borrowed, not owned, by the seller in anticipation of a price decline. If the seller can buy the asset later (to return what was borrowed) at a lower price, a profit results. If the price rises, the borrower/seller suffers a loss.

Sovereign Debt: A country's government-issued debt, priced in its native currency, which can be sold to investors in other countries.

Spreads, Maturity Spreads, Credit Spreads: Measured differences between two interest rates or yields that are being compared with each other. Spreads typically are measured between fixed income securities of the same credit quality, but different maturities (maturity spreads), or of the same maturity, but different credit quality (credit spreads).

Spread Sectors: Non-Treasury debt sectors with securities that usually trade at higher yields than comparable-maturity U.S. Treasury securities. These sectors typically trade at higher yields than Treasuries because they usually have relatively higher credit risk and/or more risk of prepayment.

Spread Widening, Tightening: Changes in spreads that reflect changes in relative value, with spread widening usually indicating relative price depreciation and spread tightening indicating relative price appreciation.

Swaps: Two-party contracts entered into primarily by institutional investors for periods ranging from a few weeks to more than one year. In a standard “swap” transaction, two parties agree to exchange the returns (or differentials in rates of return) earned or realized on particular predetermined investments or instruments.

Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS): Inflation-linked debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury.

Technical Factors: Market price behavior, trends, volume, and momentum data used in determining asset value.

U.S. Treasury securities: Debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury and backed by the direct "full faith and credit" pledge of the U.S. government. Treasury securities include bills (maturing in one year or less), notes (maturing in two to 10 years) and bonds (maturing in more than 10 years). They are generally considered among the highest quality and most liquid securities in the world.

Variable-Rate Demand Notes (VRDNs): Short-term debt securities that track market interest rates using periodic (daily, weekly, monthly, or quarterly) interest rate adjustments.

Weighted Average Life to Maturity: The average time in years to receive the principal repayments.

Yield: A rate of return for bonds and other fixed-income securities. There are several types of yields and yield calculations.

Yield Curve: A line graph showing the yields of fixed income securities from a single sector from a range of different maturities at a single point in time.

Yield to Maturity: A common performance calculation for fixed income securities, which takes into account total annual interest payments, the purchase price, the redemption value, and the amount of time remaining until maturity.

Real Yield to Maturity: Yield to maturity minus any "inflation premium" that had been added/priced in.

Zero-Coupon Securities: Debt securities that are sold at a deep discount then redeemed for their full face value at maturity.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Football Returns, Ad Dollars Pour In
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

NBCUniversal (CMCSA) already sold all of its advertising inventory for Super Bowl LX. (00:25) American Eagle Outfitters (AEO) soars as Sydney Sweeney campaign pays off. (01:51) Gold reaches another record high. (02:51)

This is an abridged transcript.

The NFL season kicks off tonight with the reigning SuperBowl champions versus America’s team.

The Philadelphia Eagles will host the Dallas Cowboys in a game that is far too late for me to watch with an 8:20 kickoff on NBC and Peacock.

Advertisers have obviously been getting ready for the start of the season as NBCUniversal confirmed on Wednesday that spots for the NFL season are 90% sold out and the 2026 Super Bowl is already fully booked.

The Comcast (NASDAQ:CMCSA) property will air tonight’s opening matchup as well as Sunday Night FootBall broadcasts and Super Bowl LX in early February.

After tonight, Thursday Night Football will stream on Prime Video (AMZN).

"The unprecedented demand from advertisers across the Super Bowl and our 20th season of Sunday Night Football has made this upcoming NFL season our highest grossing to date," NBC Executive Vice President Peter Lazarus said in a media statement.

Football and betting also go hand-in-hand. Americans are expected to place an estimated $30 billion in legal sports bets on the 2025 NFL season through U.S. sportsbooks such as DraftKings (DKNG), FanDuel (FLUT), and BetMGM (MGM) (OTCPK:GMVHF).

We’re taking a deeper dive into the economics of football in today’s edition of the Wall Street Breakfast newsletter.

An ad campaign that sparked controversy has given one brand and business a reset.

American Eagle Outfitters (NYSE:AEO) is up 24% in premarket action as it credits its advertising campaign starring actress Sydney Sweeney for “record breaking new customer acquisition and brand awareness.”

The company's CEO Jay Schottenstein said in an earnings call with analysts that the reset was "across age demographics and genders."

American Eagle's (NYSE:AEO) collaboration with Kansas City Chiefs tight end Travis Kelce, announced shortly after his engagement to Taylor Swift, kept this momentum going.

"We have seen periods of very strong demand from both campaigns fueling positive traffic in August, which was up consistently throughout the month," Schottenstein said.

Jennifer Foyle, president and executive creative director of AE & Aerie, said the Sweeney and Kelce campaigns generated "a staggering 40B impressions." She added that Sweeny Signature jeans sold out within a week and some products within a day.

Gold futures notched another record high on Wednesday.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for September delivery settled +1.2% to $3,593.20/oz, another record high and the metal's sixth straight daily increase.

Front-month Comex September silver (XAGUSD:CUR) ended +1.1% to $41.542/oz, its fifth straight gain and highest settlement since September 9, 2011.

Seeking Alpha analyst Clem Chambers attributes the surge to rising geopolitical tensions as central banks and governments drive demand as a hedge against conflict.

Chambers sees gold potentially reaching $4,000-$5,000/oz, with further upside if global instability worsens. He also sees platinum and silver offering compelling supply-demand dynamics.

You can catch Clem’s full conversation on our Investing Experts podcast. I’ll leave a link in the show notes section.

Tesla robotaxi app now open to public riders

Salesforce slides despite solid Q2 results as Q3 outlook appears mixed

Nvidia's AI chips still wanted by Chinese firms despite Beijing's pressure to not buy: report

The FTSE 100 is up 0.1% and the DAX is up 0.5%.

data.title: American Century Mid Cap Value Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio Review

Equities advanced. A topsy-turvy quarter ended with stocks reaching record highs after an initial drop that carried over from the previous quarter. The rally ignited when a pause on drastic U.S. tariffs and subsequent trade deals gave investors comfort that worst-case scenarios were behind them. Investors’ increased appetite for risk sent stock markets into a fulsome rally for most of the quarter.

Technology stocks support a sustained rally. Investors retreated to low-volatility stocks early in the quarter, but large-cap technology stocks propelled the market rally during the second half of the period. Communication services stocks also supported market gains. Health care and energy were laggards due to policy headwinds and falling oil prices, respectively.

Health care dampened results. Allocation decisions in the health care sector, which broadly faced headwinds from policy and regulatory uncertainties during the period, slowed performance relative to the benchmark. An overweight in the health care equipment and supplies industry relative to the benchmark drove underperformance as holdings in the space were buffeted by tariff complications and policy challenges in the U.S.

Information technology hurt. Stock picks in information technology, along with a broad underweight to the sector relative to the benchmark, slowed results. The portfolio’s overweight to the computers and peripherals industry was driven by a stock that underperformed. Elsewhere, limited exposure to the software industry and selection among less volatile semiconductors stocks also hampered performance.

Utilities supported results. Positioning in the utilities sector helped lift relative performance. While the portfolio is overweight in the sector compared to the benchmark, not owning certain benchmark electric utilities and a lack of exposure to water utilities contributed to results during the period.

Key Contributors

Northern Trust (NTRS). This financial services company has limited expense growth and raised its net interest income guidance during the period. Shares of Northern Trust also gained on reports that it may be an acquisition target.

Oshkosh (OSK). This specialty equipment manufacturer announced strong margins on its access equipment segment and moves to mitigate tariff headwinds. Oshkosh has also begun to ramp up its contract to provide new vehicles to the U.S. Postal Service.

L3Harris Technologies (LHX). This defense contractor benefited from projections of increased spending on missiles and missile defense systems, given broad geopolitical tensions and spending outlays. L3Harris also reported that tariffs would pose few, if any, difficulties to its business.

Key Detractors

Zimmer Biomet Holdings (ZBH). This medical technology company’s shares were pressured by the prospect of higher tariffs in China and Europe. Our research indicated that Zimmer Biomet’s growth has remained slightly below industry averages, although management expects new product utilization may ramp up later this year.

Becton, Dickinson and Co. (BDX). This medical technology company’s life sciences segment was pressured both by cuts to research in the U.S. and a lack of updates on plans to sell or spin off the business. We believe Becton Dickinson also faces tariff headwinds due to its exposure to China.

Robinhood Markets (HOOD). Not owning shares in this retail trading platform dampened performance. Robinhood’s shares have advanced, benefiting from its low-fee platform, which has attracted retail investors.

Notable Trades

Prosperity Bancshares (PB). We initiated a position in this bank holding company because we believe it is a high-quality company that has a favorable deposit base and because we think it has shown restraint with its loan evaluation decisions.

Hexcel (HXL). We initiated a position in this structural materials company because we believe its shares have provided an attractive entry point, and we think the company has the potential to ramp up original equipment manufacturing over time.

Beacon Roofing Supply. We exited our position in this roofing supply company due to its acquisition by QXO.

Cardinal Health (CAH). We exited our position in this health care services company following outperformance so far this year. We redeployed funds from the sale of Cardinal Health to other opportunities that we believe presented favorable risk/reward profiles.

Portfolio Positioning

The portfolio seeks to invest in companies where we believe the valuation does not reflect the quality and normal earnings power of the company. Our process is based on individual security selection, but broad themes have emerged.

Higher-quality stocks may offer resilience. Continuing inflation and uncertain economic and policy conditions, such as tariffs, have contributed to a murky outlook for the economy and interest rates, in our view. These factors, along with ongoing geopolitical risks, have investors navigating an unsettled economic landscape. Against this backdrop, we continue to focus on companies that we believe are higher quality because of their stable revenues and profits, low debt levels, stable cash flows and predictable business models that are generally less sensitive to economic conditions.

Attractive valuations in health care. Our research has led us to several health care stocks that we think offer compelling valuations and risk/reward profiles. While regulatory and policy uncertainty has weighed on the sector, we believe some concerns are overstated, and we have identified several high-quality companies that trade at what our research has indicated are historically compelling valuations. We consider health care to be typically less cyclical than other areas of the market because the economy’s performance tends to have less impact on sector demand. We also believe health care utilization remains elevated with attractive secular drivers. While we are mindful of potential regulatory headwinds, we think many policy risks have mostly been embedded in current valuations.

Opportunities in consumer staples. With slowing global growth, we have identified select opportunities in the less cyclical consumer staples sector. Our research has indicated that despite a challenging cost inflation environment, many consumer staples companies have been generating strong returns on capital, buying back stock and growing dividends. Moreover, we believe industry consolidation has enabled companies to pass on higher costs to consumers. We think actions to offset inflation, including fewer discounts and more price hikes, should support earnings and margins.

Shifting outlook for the financials sector. After a period of turmoil for regional banks following the failure of a few banks in 2023, we believe the outlook for the industry may improve. We expect banks will face a less strenuous regulatory backdrop, including greater openness to mergers and acquisitions. After insurance companies managed to raise prices substantially in recent years, our research has indicated that pricing power has started to decelerate meaningfully. We expect this will result in more subdued profitability for the industry going forward.

Limited opportunities in consumer discretionary. Our portfolio remains underweight in the consumer discretionary sector relative to the benchmark. We believe this sector has fewer high-quality companies with durable business models. Also, given factors like inflation and diminishing personal savings, we expect continued headwinds to discretionary spending.

Goal and Strategy

Long-term capital growth and income by investing in the stocks of mid-sized companies that are believed to be undervalued.

data.title: Joby's Pullback Sets Stage For Rebound
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Joby Aviation (JOBY) is shifting from conceptual discussions to execution-oriented champion of urban air mobility. With 70% certification progress, military deals on the books, and acquisition of Blade to accelerate commercialization, Joby has first-mover advantages that are unique.

Since, my previous coverage, JOBY has dropped ~20%, and I took profits, trimming my position at levels above $20. Although near-term risks remain from regulatory to manufacturing, Joby’s multi-pronged plan positions it for huge potential upside. For patient investors with a longer time horizon, Joby is an asymmetric bet on transportation's future, with real-world progress for once catching up with early vision.

How Joby’s Triple-Thrust Strategy Is Turning Potential Into Progress

Joby’s most meaningful accomplishments in Q2 of 2025 came from certification by authorities, commercial infrastructure, and defense dual-use markets. In certification, Joby is 70% complete through on its own Stage 4 requirements and more than 50% through on FAA. The first of five TIA type inspection aircraft has moved through to final assembly, with Joby still on track for FAA pilot flight tests early in 2026. Unlike its rivals still slogging through paperwork, Joby has earned credibility with flight testing, with 76 flights tests across three geographies proving technical maturity and operational readiness.

Acquiring Blade’s passenger business completely changes Joby’s commercialization profile in radical ways. Joby no longer needs post-certification route development, with Joby having a scaled, high-end helicopter network with more than 50,000 passengers taken last year, New York dedicated vertiport lounges, and proven European routes. Merging its assets with those of Blade produces a virtually simultaneous revenue stream with asset-light operations. The acquisition jumps ahead of time-to-market in the United States and internationally and builds ﬁrst-mover beneﬁts that are challenging for one’s competitors to replicate.

Finally, defense is a low-key pillar of growth. Joby is unique among eVTOL makers to have landed at an American military base and flown piloted and unpiloted missions. The L3Harris team extends that tradition to hybrid-electric and missionized variations by riding the Department of Defense’s $9.4 billion annual request for appropriations for autonomous and hybrid aviation by FY26. To that end, dual-use design not just increases sources of revenue but can bring technology maturity sooner.

Cash Burn Slows as Smart Spending Fuels Long-Term Lift-Off

Joby ended Q2 2025 with cash and short-term investments of $991 million, bolstered by the first installment of Toyota's $250 million tranche and $41 million from the ATM facility. Cash burn for the quarter came to $112 million, maintaining full-year expectations of $500–$540 million. Significantly, cash burn was modestly lower than in Q1, with disciplined capital allocation supporting this reduction.

GAAP figures included noise around non-cash items like a $157 million mark-to-market revaluation of warrants and investment in Toyota. The $168 million of net operating costs rose only modestly quarter on quarter despite increased program spending to accelerate TIA readiness. The loss EBITDA adjusted of $132 million was greater than last year but as expected with scaling engineering, testing, and manufacturing. Lastly, the balance sheet is strong with sufficient liquidity to support at least 18 months of burn even before considering additional tranches from Toyota or revenue contributions from Blade at close.

What is impressive is not profitability tomorrow, even years from now, but self-restraint in expenditure to build out infrastructure. While speculative counterparts are all about burn profiles separate from milestone accomplishments on certification, expansion of facilities, and commercialization, Joby’s is compatible with milestone-based achievements.

Built to Scale, Priced to Fly: Joby’s Quiet Revolution in eVTOL Production

Production follows as a bottleneck, and Joby tackles this gradually. The expansion of its Marina factory will put out capacity by half to roughly two dozen annual aircraft, and its new Dayton, Ohio factory positions itself for eventual scaling horizontally to 500 per year. The 18,000-piece production output observed in July shows the first incremental gains in process maturity. Collaboration with Toyota, the most efficient carmaker on Earth, brings with it credibility as much as execution momentum to industrialization.

Cost effectiveness also derives from the design of the model. Through incorporation of Blade’s routes and infrastructure, Joby avoids years of CapEx-intensive buildout. The asset-light model of Blade guarantees margin improvement once eVTOLs substitute helicopters by virtue of reduced operating expenses as well as noise profiles. The joint venture with ANA in Japan as well as the distribution agreement with Abdul Latif Jameel in Saudi Arabia apply the cost-sharing concept on a larger scale. Joby uses partners for building out ecosystems with capture of long-term free cash flow from operations as well as sales.

Astronomical Multiples Now, But a Multi-Billion Market Awaits

By headline metrics, Joby is extremely expensive. With a market cap of nearly $12 billion, future P/S is ~49,000x on 2025 numbers and still ~407x on 2026 numbers, multiples that would be unsustainable for most companies. These stratospheric multiples reflect Joby’s pre-revenue stage and lead times to scaled commercialization.

In a still pre-revenue business, classic metrics like P/E or even EV/Sales do not work because little or no revenue exists to calculate them from. Valuations are therefore calculatedly based on future potential, a combination of future revenues, strategic milestones (like FAA certification for Joby), and market opportunity size. In short, investors are discounting today's speculative investment to pay off as dominant market share tomorrow. Joby thus trades at astronomical-seeming multiples because investors are not paying for revenue today, but for scaled operation potential in a multi-billion-dollar market.

EVTOLs for markets offer a classic case of that dynamic in play. According to Grand View Research, the market is projected to grow at a 54.9% CAGR from 2024 to 2030 and hit tens of billions of dollars in value. Growth is likely across types of propulsion, fully electric, hybrid, electric hydrogen, with fully electric being dominant. For Joby, that means that the potential addressable market isn’t niche mobility but global transport revolution, from urban air taxis to defense, logistics, and global ops.

Risk and Reward in the Skies: The Binary Bet on Joby

While advancements have been made, material risks persist. The certification process is by its nature subject to uncertainty, as though Joby is ahead of its peers, FAA delays or unexpected technical challenges might stretch out the timetable, accelerating burn and delaying commercialization. The risk of execution of scale manufacturing is high as well, reaching 500 aircraft per year will prove even Toyota’s leadership.

Additionally, you can’t ignore competition. Archer Aviation (ACHR), Lilium (OTC:LILMF), and Vertical Aerospace (EVTL) all have differentiated designs and partnerships. They don’t rival Joby’s certification maturity and infrastructure combination, but investor momentum might shift if competitors’ earlier revenue or defense wins come to fruition.

Financially, Joby is still forecasting several years of EBITDA-negative periods. Despite $991 million of liquidity, surprise overruns or slower-than-projected Blade integration could force periodic additional capital raises with dilutive risk.

Thirdly, approval from regulators and from the public is variable. Noise, safety attitudes, and local airspace policy may restrain activity at peak sites like New York. Worldwide activity is promising but will depend upon successful multilateral certification harmonization. Joby’s average-case bull case is justified, but investing is binary on scale and certification. Joby Aviation is shifting from being a visionary start-up to a structurally superior aviation platform.

Certification traction, merging with Blade, and defense diversification are all possible revenue streams, and Toyota-backed production provides scale. Purchasers at prevailing valuation are being asked to pay for optionality but receive hard execution. Risk-averse investors who are fine with short-term volatility are being offered asymmetric exposure to transportation’s future of tomorrow.

Takeaway

With rock-solid certification track record, plug-and-play commercial network with Blade, and defense contracts signed, Joby's outlook is more than promising. There are risks involved, but disciplined scale and strategic relationships offer investors a rare, asymmetric future potential in urban air mobility.

data.title: Carlyle Secured Lending: Thesis Has Played Out, But Macro Uncertainty Is A Real Risk (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

With less uncertainty surrounding interest rates now but the potential for further weakening as a result of how tariffs will impact consumers in the coming months, certain sectors remain at higher risk as a result. One in particular, the BDC sector (BIZD) remains on high alert due to lower base rates and tightening spreads.

Carlyle Secured Lending (NASDAQ:CGBD), despite risks, is one of the few BDCs I've rated a buy in recent months. And this was due to their discounted valuation, strong balance sheet, and closed merger in March which was expected to provide synergies going forward.

So far, my thesis has seemed to play out with their latest quarter showing improvements. But as previously mentioned, macro uncertainty remains, leading me to downgrade them from a buy to a hold until there's more clarity, likely over the next 6 months.

In this article, I discuss Carlyle Secured Lending's latest quarter, improvements, and why despite these, I'm downgrading them from a buy to a hold.

Previous Buy Thesis

I last covered Carlyle Secured Lending in June in an article titled: 11.5% Yield, 16% Discount, And Merger Keep Me Bullish. In Q1, CGBD's net investment income and total investment income were $0.40 per share and $54.8 million.

Despite their tight dividend coverage, their flexible balance sheet with no debt maturities until 2028, spillover, and reduced leverage were positive signs of dividend safety.

Moreover, their P/NAV ratio of just 0.82x, which was cheap in comparison to peers, was another reason I considered the BDC a buy. Since then, their price has remained relatively flat, up close to 1% compared to the S&P (SP500) which is up close to 6%.

Latest Quarter Showed Improvements

During their Q2 earnings reported at the beginning of August, CGBD saw net investment income fall to $0.39 per share, representing a decline quarter-over-quarter and from the prior year. This fell a penny from the previous quarter and declined from $0.51 on a per share basis.

Their top line managed to see an increase amounting to roughly $67.3 million, up from $54.8 million in Q1 and $58.3 million the previous year's quarter. But not to be overshadowed by the bottom line decline on a per share basis, NII actually grew in totality.

For the quarter, this was $28.2 million, up from $20.8 million in Q1 and from $26 million on an annualized basis. So, the merger close this past March seems to have made a positive impact as I expected.

Moreover, originations during the quarter reached a record $375.7 million, an increase from $180 million in the prior quarter. However, CGBD did experience a loss of $14 million in the quarter, or $0.19, as a result of markdowns on underperforming borrowers.

As a result, this impacted their net asset value, which declined $0.20 quarter-over-quarter to $16.43. Annually, NAV decline was approximately 3.1% from $16.95 the previous year's quarter. But going forward, with the merger I expect NAV to see growth over time.

For comparison purposes, this was lower than some of their peers. Monroe Capital (MRCC) saw a decline of 9.7% from $9.18 to $8.29. New Mountain Finance's decline was 4.2% from $12.74 to $12.21. Fidus Investment was the only one who actually saw growth, up less than a half percent from $19.50 to $19.57.

CGBD's total portfolio value stood at approximately $2.3 billion, up from $1.7 billion in the prior year's quarter, while their borrower count grew from 126 to 148. Investments grew from 180 to 202.

Their exposure to first-lien loans also vastly improved from 70.9% to 85.6% over the same period, giving the BDC a much more defensive portfolio.

For comparison, this growth puts them over peers like New Mountain Finance (NMFC) and Fidus Investment (FDUS) whose first-lien exposure stood at 78.0% and 81.0% at the end of their latest quarters.

Improved Non-Performing Loans

Despite losses during the quarter, CGBD's overall portfolio risk rating remained flat at 89.7%. But non-accruals improved subsequent to quarter's end due to management successfully restructuring one investment.

In Q1, non-accruals were 2.2% and 1.6% on a cost and fair value basis. These now stand at 1.3% at cost and 1.0% at fair value. Year-over-year these improved from 2.8% and 1.8%, respectively.

Balance Sheet

One of the biggest reasons I was high on Carlyle Secured Lending was their flexible balance sheet. Their first maturity isn't until December of 2028, giving them ample liquidity and flexibility to take advantage of growth opportunities.

Additionally, they upsized their credit facility, giving them total liquidity of $960 million, including $613 million in cash. So, the merger synergies seem to be having a positive impact so far.

Attractive Discount & Dividend Coverage

With their improvement, this makes CGBD's valuation even more attractive with a P/NAV ratio of just 0.85x. With tighter spreads, declining net investment income, and projected lower base rates, it's possible CGBD's headwinds could already be priced in here.

In the latest quarter, dividend coverage fell below 100% of their base dividend of $0.40, a steep decline from 128% in the previous year's quarter. And while management stated they did have spillover of $0.89 in the quarter and seemed optimistic in supporting the dividend near-term, lower base rates and the potential for further economic weakening are the main reasons for my downgrade.

I do think because of their current valuation, if they do cut the dividend, they could see less of a decline in share price in comparison to other peers like Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL) and Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), whose dividends I also think are at high risk right now.

BXSL still trades at a premium to NAV, while MSDL's P/NAV is more attractive at 0.86x, roughly in line with CGBD. So, from a yield and valuation standpoint, Carlyle Secured Lending could be viewed as attractive if you're income-focused.

Risks & Bottom Line

As stated previously, macro uncertainty remains higher and lower base rates are expected as odds recently jumped after the Fed's Jackson Hole speech. This will cause headwinds going forward. Management touched on this during earnings:

Spreads in the private credit space remain at historically tight levels and, when combined with potential Fed rate cuts, may present a headwind to near-term earnings.

Furthermore, I expect tariffs could make a larger impact in the back half of the year, which would also affect borrowers. This could cause an increase in non-accruals and payment-in-kind income. However, CGBD's management stated this impacts less than 5% of the portfolio.

While I think Carlyle Secured Lending remains attractive due to their 11.4% yield and discounted valuation, macroeconomic risks remain, at least for the near term.

Although I remain bullish as I expect merger synergies to continue to benefit the BDC long term, less than 100% dividend coverage despite spillover leads me to rate them a hold for now.

data.title: American Century International Growth Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio Review

Markets demonstrated resilience. Stocks broadly finished the second quarter higher despite geopolitical tensions, tariff uncertainties and significant market volatility. Artificial intelligence and technology experienced a strong rebound as fears around DeepSeek faded. Outside the U.S., falling interest rates were a tailwind, while the weakening U.S. dollar poses risks and opportunities for non-U.S. investors.

The divergence between soft and hard data continued to grow. Shifting U.S. tariff policies created a high degree of uncertainty, which weighed on sentiment indicators. However, hard data remained resilient, particularly in employment and corporate earnings, as non-U.S. companies reported above-average earnings surprises. Although additional trade agreements are expected, the outlook remains murky.

Industrials holdings were a source of strength. Electrical equipment industry holding Siemens Energy was a key contributor compared with the benchmark within the industrials sector after the company announced strong results, which beat expectations, and management raised guidance for the year. Mitsubishi Heavy Industries also provided a boost as investors responded favorably to the company's strong orders and improving profitability.

Positioning within the energy sector proved beneficial. A lack of exposure to underperforming benchmark names involved in oil, gas and consumable fuels added to performance compared with the benchmark. Within the energy equipment and services industry, Saipem was a notable contributor.

Financials holdings weighed on relative performance. Among capital markets industry holdings, London Stock Exchange Group reported lower-than-expected revenue and currency exchange headwinds. Partners Group Holding also detracted compared with the benchmark. We sold the position due to decelerating earnings growth. Within banking, Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group hindered performance with lower-than-expected net income and tariff impact on interest rate expectations.

Key Contributors

Siemens Energy. The Germany-based energy company released second-quarter results, which showed orders, sales and earnings soundly beat expectations for the period. The company also significantly raised its fiscal year guidance.

Ryohin Keikaku. This household products retailer has benefited from an improved economic outlook in Japan. The company’s stock performed strongly, supported by robust single-store sales.

Mitsubishi Heavy Industries. This manufacturer of ships, industrial machinery and aircraft reported strong orders but weaker earnings and conservative guidance due to capacity constraints and project timing. However, investors responded favorably to the company’s strong order backlog and its improving profitability.

Key Detractors

AstraZeneca. Shares of this British-Swedish multinational pharmaceuticals manufacturer fell as investors worried about the potential effects of the broad U.S. tariff announcement on products sold in the U.S. The company’s oncology drug sales also slightly missed expectations due in part to the U.S. Medicare Part D redesign.

London Stock Exchange Group. The financial exchange operator reported lower-than-expected revenue, and it faced foreign exchange headwinds. However, growth in the data and analytics segment continues to improve, which we believe positions the company well for future margin expansion.

Keyence. This Japan-based automation company missed profit and revenue targets in the latest quarter. U.S. tariff fears also weighed on its share price.

Notable Trades

Spotify Technology. We initiated a position in the popular streaming service as the company has seen strong user growth, which has contributed to its robust operating income.

National Grid. We added a position in this U.K.-based utility company, encouraged by management’s focus on its energy transmission portfolio. We believe National Grid’s growth is supported by continued investments in the electrical grid.

UBS Group. This Swiss financial services company faces potential headwinds from proposed legislation that would make capital requirements significantly more stringent in Switzerland. We exited the position.

Partners Group Holding. Concerns about decelerating earnings growth led us to exit our position in this private equity firm. We redeployed the capital into names with more compelling risk/reward profiles.

Portfolio Positioning

The portfolio continues to invest in companies we believe are strong and improving but whose share price performance does not fully reflect these factors. Our process is based on individual security selection, but broad themes have emerged.

European valuations remain attractive with select opportunities. The pace of interest rate cuts in European economies may stimulate accelerated growth in those regions as they emerge from economic lows. This creates a tailwind for rate-sensitive areas including banks, housing and infrastructure. NATO-driven increases to European defense budgets create opportunities in traditional defense and cybersecurity.

Digital transformation supports information technology positions. The acceleration of digitalization is benefiting technology holdings exposed to cloud computing, automation, digital payments and information technology services growth. Japan-based companies, in particular, are investing heavily to increase efficiency and profits as they seek to catch up with their international competitors.

AI integration enables growth. Artificial intelligence investment has started to migrate to the application layer as data-centric companies integrate their AI-enabled features to capture market share and create earnings growth inflections.

Modern vehicles create demand for analog semiconductors. Analog chipmakers have experienced a rebound in order volumes after a destocking period that caused weaker sales. A key driver of this trend is the increasing semiconductor content in electric vehicles, which require significantly more chips than traditional internal combustion engine vehicles. This shift in vehicle technology is fueling sustained demand growth.

Supply chain normalization has accelerated civil aircraft production. Delays due to jet engine shortages and supplier disruptions are resolving, allowing manufacturers to ramp up aircraft output and deliver more planes. This recovery is timely, as global air traffic remains strong and demand for travel continues to support the civil aviation market.

Goal and Strategy

Long-term capital growth by investing primarily in large companies in developed countries outside the U.S.

data.title: Kraft Heinz Offers A Decent, Potentially Safe Dividend And Renewed Sales Growth Post-Split
data.description:
Investment thesis: On the surface, Kraft Heinz (NASDAQ:KHC) is having yet another bad year, with a massive Q2 loss, mostly driven by an asset write-down. It has been the story with this company for some years now, prompting Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) (BRK.B) to announce that it is giving up on seeing its long-term investment in this company pay off. Given that the stock is down about 60% from its all-time highs, it is understandable why Berkshire would consider this investment a permanent loss for the company. I do not see this stock ever reaching all-time highs again. It would take a rally of about 300% for that to happen, and there is simply no market scenario, aside from a powerful inflationary trend, that would produce such a gain in this stock. However, with a dividend nearing 6%, which seems secure, and decent prospects for its stock price to see moderate gains post-split, there is an argument to be made for this company to be a good investment opportunity, especially as its share price is being battered by a skeptical market.

Kraft Heinz continues to underwhelm in terms of its financial performance, but there are some decent trends

In the second quarter of the year, the dominant feature of the company's financial result was a $9.3 billion asset write-down. The effect of the write-down on its results was towering over everything else. Net income for the first six months of the year came in at a loss of $7.11 billion. Subtracting the write-down from the results, Kraft Heinz would have seen net income of $2.2 billion in H1.

The write-down is not the only negative aspect of the report by any means. Sales are down to $12.35 billion in the first half of the year, compared with $12.89 billion in the first half of 2024. It is a 3% decline for the period, which has become a steady feature of its financial results. It is arguably the problem that the recent push to split the company seeks to address.

Other results of note that are relevant in considering our options as investors in the face of the plan to split the company into two include interest costs that came in at $469 million for the first half of the year. It is a slight 3% increase on the same period in 2024. Interest costs made up 3.8% of revenues, which is, in my view, one of the most consequential aspects of the company's financial profile. I find any interest/revenue ratio that approaches 5% to be problematic, because it raises the prospect of a vicious cycle forming, where a company may have to raise external financial resources just to service the debt. Kraft Heinz is not there just yet, but it is starting to inch toward my zone of discomfort in this regard. Net cash from operating activities increased by $216 million for the latest quarter compared with the same quarter from a year earlier to $1.9 billion, which is a sign of improving financial resilience, because it reduces the need to issue debt or dilute its shares. It also makes it less likely that a cut in its dividend payments will be needed.

Potential benefits of the coming company split

The details of the split are not 100% clear yet, but its major brands will be divided into a company that will focus on grocery-related goods such as Oscar Mayer, Kraft Singles, and Lunchables, and for now, it is referred to as "North American Grocery Co." The other company will focus on the Heinz brand, Philadelphia, and Kraft Mac & Cheese, and it is currently referred to as "Global Taste Elevation Co." Warren Buffett, whose Berkshire Hathaway company owns over a quarter of the company's shares, came out against the split, arguing that it will not fix the company's long-term fundamental problems. Based on the details, I partially have to agree with that assessment. There is not a great deal of streamlining that can occur as a result of the split. The only difference is that one focuses on the North American market, as the name suggests, while the other has a more international sales profile.

There is arguably a viable argument in having one company with a global reach mindset while the other focuses on the North American market. Culinary preferences differ across markets. With the North American Grocery Co. focused on catering to North American consumer preferences, it could at the very least stem the steady sales decline we have seen from this company. The Global Taste Elevation company, on the other hand, with an outsized international presence, reportedly with a fifth of its current sales coming from the expanding developing world market, could tailor its products more toward catering to such markets, where there are arguably more opportunities. It is worth mentioning that so far this year, emerging markets have been the only area of growth for the company.

It should also be noted that its sales in the developed world outside of North America declined at a far lesser pace this year compared with its North American sales. If a North America-oriented company can stem that decline by being more focused on this market, and a more global-oriented company can focus on tapping international growth markets, current shareholders might see themselves benefiting in the long term. In the next few months, Kraft Heinz shares will trade rather erratically, given the added uncertainty on top of a bad decade for long-term shareholders, but once the dust settles, I believe there will be a clearer picture of a net benefit to the plan to split the company.

Investment implications

Warren Buffett can be forgiven for expressing frustration & disappointment with this investment, since the new company was born out of the Kraft Heinz merger.

Some bought shares in the first few years after the merger, believing in the project that was, after all, backed by Warren Buffett, and as a consequence, they fared even worse than he did. Long-term investors who have been along for the unpleasant ride, as well as the broader market, will probably react initially to this latest news by judging it as nothing more than a desperate attempt at salvaging an otherwise unsuccessful merger. My view is that it helps to see the company's performance more in terms of how it did in the current decade than how it performed overall since the merger.

As we can see, so far this decade, Kraft Heinz has performed relatively well in terms of investor returns relative to industry peers when the effect of dividends being reinvested in the company is included. The segment overall has not seen a great decade so far, but within its investment category, Kraft Heinz is no longer a laggard.

Looking at its current forward P/E ratio of about 10, as well as other financial metrics, such as an improving cash flow, combined with potentially improved long-term sales prospects for the two new companies, I find Kraft Heinz to be a potential outperformer post-split. Campbell Soup (CPB), with a similar forward P/E ratio and an inferior dividend currently at under 5%, for instance, is also struggling with sales growth, so Kraft Heinz is arguably trading at valuations that do not currently factor in much potential upside.

  • Risks to my thesis

I believe that the plan of the split, as I see it, with one company geared toward consolidating its North American sales while the other company will be geared toward seeking to take advantage of the growing market in the developing world, has merit. The main risk, as I see it, is in execution. Clearly, Kraft Heinz already has a footprint in the developing world that can serve as a platform for achieving higher sales growth, but for success to be achieved, all opportunities and existing assets must be fully taken advantage of, which requires a good understanding of the target markets. Stemming the loss of sales in the North American market will be the more formidable task of the second company. It can be achieved, but it will take good decisions to get the new company to meet that objective.

  • A relatively safe & generous dividend, with prospects of selling shares at a higher price some years down the road

I already had a significant position in Kraft Heinz stock before news of the plan to split the company in two emerged. My reasoning behind investing was that it offers a decent dividend, currently at over 5.7% on a forward basis, while the downside risk to its share price is somewhat limited, given its valuation and stable financial performance, recent write-downs notwithstanding. I do not doubt that the market will greet the news of the split with some skepticism, leading to a downward trend in the company's share price in the next few months, especially given the weight of Warren Buffett's statement in this regard.

I see this as a buying opportunity, given that the company's financial performance is stable and the split does have the potential to create some upside to its share price in the longer term. While there is some risk that the split will not turn out to be a fix to its current challenge of improving sales, the downside to its share price in the longer term is somewhat limited, and if the dividend stays in place, it could more than offset a moderate loss in share price in the longer term. This is therefore an investment opportunity with some moderate upside relative to the overall market, while the potential downside risk seems somewhat limited.

data.title: American Century Intermediate-Term Tax-Free Bond Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Munis declined modestly. Interest rate volatility, federal spending cuts and tax policy uncertainty drove municipal bond (muni) returns lower early in the quarter. Eventually, tax policy clarity and waning volatility helped spark a June rebound, which offset some earlier muni market losses. But the broad investment-grade municipal bond market remained in negative territory for the quarter.

Economy contracted slightly. The U.S. economy declined 0.5% (annualized) in the first quarter, down from 2.4% in the fourth quarter. A surge in imports from companies stockpiling goods in response to pending tariffs largely drove the downturn. Meanwhile, second-quarter data showed manufacturing rose to a three-year high, job growth was better than expected and the unemployment rate ticked lower.

Munis underperformed Treasuries. While Treasury yields declined across the curve, municipal bond yields were mixed. Yields on shorter-maturity munis declined, while yields on longer-maturity munis rose. Overall, munis underperformed Treasuries. Additionally, general obligation (GO)) bonds fared better than revenue bonds, and shorter-maturity bonds generally outperformed longer-maturity issues.

Muni supply surged again. Second-quarter municipal bond supply jumped to more than $161 billion, compared to $111 billion in March. As expected, issuers continued to issue bonds ahead of potential tax policy changes in the federal budget deal. By quarter-end, it was clear that Congress would preserve the tax-exempt status of munis.

States’ spending to flatten. Most state budgets for fiscal 2026 project flat growth in general fund spending. Governors expect fewer one-time expenditures from surplus funds, limited recurring spending increases and slow growth in general fund revenue. Federal budget changes to Medicaid shift costs to states, but the extended timeline for implementation may offset funding gaps.

High-yield munis underperformed. Municipal credit spreads widened marginally for the quarter, more significantly among high-yield securities. High-yield municipal bond returns declined and underperformed investment-grade munis for the quarter. Meanwhile, ratings upgrades to downgrades were even, and defaults declined.

Portfolio Performance Review

Security selection detracted. Security selection was the main driver of underperformance compared with the benchmark, largely due to choices in the tax increment, special assessment and dedicated tax sectors. Positive effects from security selection among charter schools and private universities helped offset some negative results.

Sector allocations modestly weighed on results. Underweight positions relative to the benchmark in state GO bonds and prerefunded securities and an overweight in charter schools detracted from performance compared with the benchmark. These allocations more than offset positive allocation effects from a position in the energy prepay sector and underweights in the dedicated tax and tax increment sectors.

Duration aided performance. Our duration positioning, which was slightly longer than the benchmark, boosted relative performance. Roll-down effects also aided results.

Positioning for the Future

Amid uncertainties, economy may slow. We still believe weak growth is a likely near-term scenario, mostly due to still restrictive monetary policy and tariff uncertainties. Recent upbeat employment and manufacturing data suggest a recession has become less likely. And if the trade and geopolitical backdrops improve, we believe a shift to stronger growth would be a mounting possibility.

Muni issuance may ease. We expect the supply of municipal securities to drive the growth of the asset class, which has lagged other fixed-income areas. The recently passed federal budget preserved the tax-exempt status of munis, removing a key market uncertainty and driver of strong year-to-date issuance. Accordingly, muni issuance may slow down through year-end.

Remaining selective. Tight valuations are keeping us cautious toward spreads. As fiscal policies take shape, we expect the health care and state sectors to face pressures. Meanwhile, demographic changes and fluid federal policies may present headwinds to higher education bonds. In this environment, we favor essential services and other sectors removed from federal budget effects.

Goal and Strategy

Total return and current income that is exempt from federal income taxes through investment-grade, intermediate-term municipal bonds.
